g.data-narration: Welcome to Scope of Variables in OpenMP. This fundamental concept determines whether your parallel code is correct or buggy.
Incorrect variable scoping causes race conditions and data corruption. These bugs are extremely difficult to debug since they produce non-deterministic results.
g.text: Incorrect variable scoping is the #1 source of bugs in OpenMP programs:
Race Conditions • Data Corruption • Non-deterministic Results

g.data-narration: Variable scope determines which threads can access a variable and whether access is shared or private. OpenMP provides clauses to control this behavior.
g.text: Variable SCOPE determines which threads can access a variable and whether access is:

g.data-narration: Shared variables exist in one memory location. All threads read and write to this single address. This is the default for variables declared outside the parallel region.
g.text: • All threads access the SAME memory address
• Default for variables declared OUTSIDE parallel region
Requires synchronization (critical/atomic) to avoid race conditions

g.data-narration: Private variables create a separate copy for each thread. Each thread operates on its local copy without interference, eliminating race conditions.
g.text: • Each thread operates on its own local copy
• Default for variables declared INSIDE parallel region
No synchronization needed - eliminates race conditions!

g.data-narration: The private clause explicitly declares a variable private. Inside the region, each thread gets an uninitialized copy. The original variable's value is unchanged after.
g.text: Each thread gets UNINITIALIZED copy • Original unchanged after region

g.data-narration: Firstprivate initializes each thread's private copy with the original variable's value before the parallel region. Useful when you need the starting value.
Lastprivate updates the original variable with the value from the last sequential iteration after the parallel region. Essential for capturing final loop results.
The shared clause explicitly declares variables as shared. The default clause sets the default scope for unlisted variables in the parallel region.
Always use default(none) and explicitly specify scope for every variable. This prevents bugs from implicit defaults and is the gold standard for safe OpenMP code.
g.text: default(none) forces you to explicitly specify the scope for EVERY variable!
This prevents accidental bugs from implicit default scoping rules.

g.data-narration: Default rules: variables outside parallel region are shared by default. Variables declared inside are private. Loop indices are always private in for directives.
g.text: ⚡ Loop index in #pragma omp for is ALWAYS private, regardless of declaration!

g.data-narration: The reduction clause combines private copies with a specified operator. It creates private copies, computes locally, then combines results atomically at the end.
g.text: Creates private copy per thread → Computes locally → Combines at end

g.data-narration: Watch how reduction works: each thread has a private sum initialized to 0. They compute partial sums, then OpenMP atomically combines them into the final result.
Avoid these common mistakes: race conditions on shared variables, uninitialized private variables, forgetting lastprivate, and relying on implicit defaults.
g.text: Need final loop value after region

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: shared is default outside parallel region, private is default inside. Use firstprivate for initialization, lastprivate for final values, and reduction for accumulations.
Exam tips: memorize default rules, know firstprivate vs lastprivate difference, use reduction for accumulation, always use default(none), and spot race conditions in code.
